examples.title: Basic Thread Creation and Join
examples.description: Demonstrates creating a new thread and waiting for it to complete using pseudocode
examples.code:
FUNCTION main()
  threadHandle = CREATE_THREAD(workerFunction, arg)
  PRINT "Main thread continuing..."
  JOIN_THREAD(threadHandle)  // Wait for thread to finish
  PRINT "Thread completed"
END FUNCTION

FUNCTION workerFunction(arg)
  PRINT "Worker thread executing with arg: " + arg
  FOR i = 1 TO 5
    PRINT "Worker: " + i
  END FOR
  RETURN 0
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the basic pattern of thread creation using pthread_create() equivalent. The main thread creates a worker thread that executes independently, while the main thread continues. JOIN_THREAD blocks until the worker thread completes.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for creation, O(n) for worker execution

examples.title: POSIX Thread Creation Example
examples.description: C program demonstrating pthread creation, passing arguments, and joining threads
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates POSIX threads (pthread). pthread_create() creates a new thread executing threadFunction. Each thread receives a unique argument. pthread_join() makes the main thread wait for each thread to complete. The threads execute concurrently, with output interleaved based on OS scheduling.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for thread execution where n is iteration count
examples.spaceComplexity: O(stack_size) per thread + O(1) for thread handles

examples.title: Python Threading with Thread Class
examples.description: Python program showing thread creation using the threading module with synchronization
examples.code:
import threading
import time

shared_counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()

def worker(thread_id, iterations):
    global shared_counter
    for i in range(iterations):
        with lock:  # Acquire lock before modifying shared data
            current = shared_counter
            time.sleep(0.001)  # Simulate some work
            shared_counter = current + 1
        time.sleep(0.001)
    print(f"Thread {thread_id} finished")

# Create and start threads
threads = []
for i in range(4):
    t = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(i, 10))
    threads.append(t)
    t.start()

# Wait for all threads to complete
for t in threads:
    t.join()

print(f"Final counter value: {shared_counter} (expected: 40)")
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates threading with synchronization. The lock ensures mutual exclusion when modifying shared_counter. Without the lock, race conditions would cause lost updates. Thread.start() begins execution, and Thread.join() waits for completion. This demonstrates why synchronization is critical in multithreaded programs.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * iterations) where n is number of threads
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for thread objects + O(1) for shared data

examples.title: Thread Pool Implementation
examples.description: Pseudocode showing how a thread pool works with a task queue
examples.code:
CLASS ThreadPool
  queue taskQueue
  list workerThreads
  int poolSize
  boolean shutdownFlag
  
  CONSTRUCTOR ThreadPool(size)
    poolSize = size
    FOR i = 1 TO size
      workerThreads.add(CREATE_THREAD(workerFunction, this))
    END FOR
  END CONSTRUCTOR
  
  METHOD submitTask(task)
    taskQueue.enqueue(task)
  END METHOD
  
  FUNCTION workerFunction(pool)
    WHILE NOT pool.shutdownFlag
      task = pool.taskQueue.dequeue(TIMEOUT)  // Wait for task
      IF task != NULL THEN
        task.execute()
      END IF
    END WHILE
  END FUNCTION
  
  METHOD shutdown()
    shutdownFlag = TRUE
    FOR EACH thread IN workerThreads
      JOIN_THREAD(thread)
    END FOR
  END METHOD
END CLASS
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows a thread pool implementation. Instead of creating a new thread for each task, a fixed pool of worker threads wait on a task queue. When tasks are submitted, idle workers pick them up. This reduces thread creation overhead and limits resource usage. The shutdown method gracefully terminates all workers.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for task submission, O(task_time) for execution
examples.spaceComplexity: O(pool_size) for worker threads + O(queue_size) for tasks
